coredata.dc:title: Olive pomace-derived graphene quantum dots decorated with iron oxide nanoparticles for efficient malathion removal from environmental water: Theoretical and experimental studies

coredata.dc:description: With increasing water pollution, research has focused on developing green, cost-effective, and sustainable materials for efficient water treatment. In this study, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) derived from olive pomace were synthesized via a simple hydrothermal method and decorated with iron oxide nanoparticles (Fe3O4) to form GQDs@Fe3O4 nanocomposites for malathion removal from environmental water. The physicochemical properties of these nanocomposites were characterized using SEM, FTIR, XRD, UV–Vis spectroscopy, and nitrogen adsorption-desorption techniques. The adsorption efficiency of the synthesized adsorbent was evaluated by measuring its adsorption capacity and removal efficiency, considering various parameters such as initial pH, initial concentration, temperature, adsorbent dosage, contact time, and salt concentration. Adsorption behavior was analyzed using kinetic and isothermal models, revealing that the Freundlich isotherm (R2 = 0.9996) and the pseudo-first-order kinetic model (R2 = 0.9998) provided the best fit for the malathion adsorption process. Under optimal conditions (5 mg L−1 malathion, 30 mg adsorbent, 210 min), a maximum removal efficiency of 87.19 ± 4.47 % was achieved at 298.15 K, increasing to 97.24 ± 2.19 % at 323.15 K. When applied to real water samples from river and dam sources, the nanocomposites maintained a removal efficiency of 73–67 %, demonstrating their practical applicability. FTIR analysis confirmed the presence of malathion on the adsorbent surface post-adsorption, providing insights into the adsorption mechanism, which was further investigated through theoretical calculations. These findings highlight the high adsorption capacity, rapid removal efficiency, and reusability of GQDs@Fe3O4 nanocomposites, demonstrating their potential as an environmentally sustainable and cost-effective solution for pesticide removal from aquatic environments.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: With increasing water pollution, research has focused on developing green, cost-effective, and sustainable materials for efficient water treatment. In this study, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) derived from olive pomace were synthesized via a simple hydrothermal method and decorated with iron oxide nanoparticles (Fe3O4) to form GQDs@Fe3O4 nanocomposites for malathion removal from environmental water. The physicochemical properties of these nanocomposites were characterized using SEM, FTIR, XRD, UV–Vis spectroscopy, and nitrogen adsorption-desorption techniques. The adsorption efficiency of the synthesized adsorbent was evaluated by measuring its adsorption capacity and removal efficiency, considering various parameters such as initial pH, initial concentration, temperature, adsorbent dosage, contact time, and salt concentration. Adsorption behavior was analyzed using kinetic and isothermal models, revealing that the Freundlich isotherm (R2 = 0.9996) and the pseudo-first-order kinetic model (R2 = 0.9998) provided the best fit for the malathion adsorption process. Under optimal conditions (5 mg L−1 malathion, 30 mg adsorbent, 210 min), a maximum removal efficiency of 87.19 ± 4.47 % was achieved at 298.15 K, increasing to 97.24 ± 2.19 % at 323.15 K. When applied to real water samples from river and dam sources, the nanocomposites maintained a removal efficiency of 73–67 %, demonstrating their practical applicability. FTIR analysis confirmed the presence of malathion on the adsorbent surface post-adsorption, providing insights into the adsorption mechanism, which was further investigated through theoretical calculations. These findings highlight the high adsorption capacity, rapid removal efficiency, and reusability of GQDs@Fe3O4 nanocomposites, demonstrating their potential as an environmentally sustainable and cost-effective solution for pesticide removal from aquatic environments.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The excessive use of pesticides in agriculture poses significant risks to human health and ecosystems [1]. Persistent compounds such as organochlorines, organophosphates, and carbamates are linked to severe health issues, including cancer, hormonal disruptions, cytotoxicity, teratogenicity, allergies, and congenital anomalies [2,3]. Pesticides present health concerns to humans through direct exposure in agricultural, domestic, and professional environments, as well as indirectly through contaminated air, water, soil, and food [4]. They deteriorate by multiple physical and microbiological mechanisms in the environment, including light, temperature, moisture, oxygen, and microbial activity [5,6]. They may be conveyed to non-target regions via adsorption, leaching, volatilization, or surface runoff during precipitation, resulting in the pollution of groundwater and surface water [6]. Consequently, the elimination of these deleterious toxic chemical species from the environmental system has proven to be a formidable challenge for researchers and the government in recent years.

Various physical, chemical, and biological techniques have been employed to remove pesticides from water and wastewater. These methods include membrane filtration, solvent extraction, chemical precipitation, evaporation, electrochemical treatment, ion exchange, advanced oxidation processes, adsorption, biosorption, nanofiltration, reverse osmosis, biological oxidation, ozonation, photocatalysis, and membrane technology [7–14]. However, many of these techniques have significant limitations. One major challenge is the production of toxic sludge, which requires additional treatment and disposal, posing both environmental and economic concerns. Additionally, certain methods are prone to fouling, reducing their efficiency and increasing maintenance costs, as seen in membrane filtration, where fouling and retentate disposal present critical environmental issues. Moreover, some techniques require high energy inputs and expensive reagents, making them less economically viable for large-scale applications, as observed in advanced oxidation and photocatalysis [15,16]. In contrast, adsorption offers several advantages, including cost-effectiveness, operational simplicity, and high removal efficiency at low pollutant concentrations [17]. Furthermore, adsorption does not generate harmful byproducts or require complex post-treatment processes, making it a more environmentally sustainable approach to wastewater treatment [18]. However, a key limitation is the separation of adsorbents in large-scale applications, which can be addressed by employing specialized magnetic adsorbents to enhance separation efficiency [19].

As a result, various nanomaterials have been extensively utilized in environmental monitoring and contaminant removal, including carbon-based materials [e.g., graphene, graphene oxide (GO), reduced GO (rGO), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon nanotubes (CNTs)] [20,21], porous organic frameworks [e.g., metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), covalent organic frameworks (COFs), and zeolitic imidazolate framework (ZIF)] [22–24], Maxene [25], and molecularly imprinted polymers [26,27]. Among these, GQDs have attracted considerable attention for pollutant identification and removal, representing the latest advancements in carbon nanomaterial-based adsorption research. GQDs exhibit remarkable physicochemical properties due to quantum confinement and edge effects. These nanoscale graphene fragments, typically ranging from 1 to 20 nm, are predominantly composed of sp2-hybridized carbon [28]. These properties make them highly suitable for catalytic degradation of organic pollutants, pesticide adsorption, and contaminant filtration. For example, Agarwal et al. synthesized GQDs using a microwave-assisted hydrothermal technique and employed them as adsorbents to effectively remove the toxic carbamate insecticide oxamyl from aqueous solutions. The optimal conditions for maximum oxamyl adsorption were found to be a pH of 8, an adsorbent dosage of 0.6 g, and a contact time of 25 min. Under these conditions, the GQDs achieved a significant adsorption capacity of 125 mg g−1 for the oxamyl insecticide pollutant. Rabeie and colleagues developed nanocomposites with various morphologies, including rod-like, diamond-like, and spindle-like structures, using GQDs and iron (Fe) for the removal of dyes and pharmaceuticals. The adsorption process followed the Langmuir isotherm and pseudo-second-order kinetics, exhibiting remarkable adsorption capacities for Acid Blue 92, tetracycline, and doxycycline (617, 672, and 796 mg g−1, respectively) [29]. In a related study, the same research team synthesized a ternary nanostructure composed of titania (TiO₂) decorated with GQDs and ZIF-8 (TiO₂/GQD/ZIF-8) using a hierarchical approach for the photocatalytic degradation of doxycycline and dyes under visible light irradiation [30]. In a separate study, three types of chitosan (CS)-based hydrogel beads incorporating varying amounts of Fe3O4-GQD nanocomposites (CS/GQD-Fe3O4) were synthesized for the removal of chlorpyrifos from aqueous solutions [31]. Batch adsorption experiments revealed that among these nanocomposite hydrogel beads, the CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (10) formulation exhibited the highest adsorption efficiency, reaching approximately 99.997 % under optimal conditions determined by response surface methodology [31].

Despite extensive research on nanomaterial-based adsorption, there remains a critical need for sustainable, cost-effective, and high-performance adsorbents for pesticide removal. In this study, we introduce a novel biomass-derived GQDs decorated with iron oxide (Fe3O4) nanoparticles (denoted as GQDs@Fe3O4), synthesized from olive pomace waste, an abundant and eco-friendly precursor. This approach not only promotes waste valorization but also offers a viable and sustainable alternative to conventional carbon-based adsorbents. Additionally, it addresses the separation challenge by leveraging the magnetic properties of the composite. The GQDs@Fe3O4 nanocomposite demonstrates high adsorption efficiency, magnetic separability, and excellent reusability, making it a promising material for practical water treatment applications. The resulting nanocomposites were characterized using various techniques, including Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) spectroscopy, scanning electron microscopy (SEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), and Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) analysis. The initial phase of the study involves investigating the impact of key parameters, such as initial concentration, pH, and contact time, on adsorption performance. The adsorption of malathion insecticide onto GQDs@Fe3O4 nanocomposites was evaluated using isotherm, thermodynamic, and kinetic models. The second phase focuses on assessing the efficacy of GQDs@Fe3O4 nanocomposites as a robust adsorption medium for malathion extraction from water, based on theoretical research.

This research study utilized high-purity reagents. [(dimethoxyphosphorothioyl)sulfanyl] butanedioate diethyl (Malathion, C₁₀H₁₉O₆PS₂, ~100 %), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 98 %), potassium chloride (KCl, 99 %), sodium chloride (NaCl, ≥99.0 %), calcium chloride (CaCl₂, ≥97 %), ferric chloride (FeCl3, 97 %), ferrous chloride (FeCl2, 98 %), ethanol (C2H5OH, 99.9 %) and acetone (C₃H₆O, 99.5 %) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Germany). Furthermore, hydrochloric acid (HCl, 37 %) was sourced from Lobachemie (India).

The synthesis of GQDs@Fe3O4 was carried out in three distinct stages. Firstly, GQDs were synthesized from olive pomace using a hydrothermal approach (Scheme 1 ). During this procedure, the olive pomace was carefully rinsed with deionized (DI) water and subsequently dried at 90 °C for 12 h. The dried olive pomace was ground until it formed a fine powder. Subsequently, 1.0 g of the olive pomace powder was dispersed in 100 mL of DI water and transferred to an autoclave. The autoclave was subsequently heated to 200 °C and sustained at that temperature for 8 to 12 h. Upon completion of the reaction, the solution was permitted to cool to ambient temperature. The resulting dark brown solution was filtered using Whatman filter paper (pore size of 0.22 μm) to remove any insoluble carbon products. The purified dark brown GQDs were dried at 80 °C in a hot air oven and subsequently refrigerated for structural characterization studies.

Secondly, Fe3O4 nanoparticles were synthesized via a co-precipitation method. Briefly, a mixture of 50 mL of 1 M FeCl2 and 50 mL of 1.8 M FeCl3 was stirred until homogenized. The temperature was then raised to 35 ± 5 °C, and the pH was adjusted to 12 by adding 6 M NaOH dropwise under continuous stirring. The mixture was vigorously stirred at 60 ± 5 °C for 2 h, then allowed to cool to room temperature. The resulting black precipitates were washed thoroughly with DI water until neutralization, followed by a final wash with ethanol. Finally, the solid product were dried in an oven at 75 °C for 24 h.

Lastly, the coordination between GQDs and Fe3O4 was initiated through the sonication of 1 g of Fe3O4 in 5 mL of water for 15 min. Subsequently, 20 mL of the GQDs suspension was introduced into the flask containing the magnetic nanoparticles, and the mixture was mechanically agitated for 48 h at 60 °C. The GQDs-modified Fe3O4 was subsequently isolated using magnetic separation. The acquired material was rigorously rinsed with deionized water and ethanol (3 × 10 mL) and subsequently dried under vacuum conditions.

A comprehensive analysis of the surface chemical composition and interactions within GQDs, Fe3O4, and GQDs@Fe3O4 samples was conducted using various advanced analytical methods. Attenuated total reflection (ATR) FTIR spectroscopy was employed using the vertex 70 Bruker equipment to examine the samples across a spectral range of 4000 to 400 cm−1, providing detailed insights into their surface chemical features. The crystalline structure of the samples was examined using XRD conducted on a Bruker diffractometer (Phillips X'pert-pro model) with Cu Kα (λ = 1.54051 Å) radiation and a step size of 0.017° per second. UV–Vis spectra were acquired using a Thermo Scientific™ Spectrophotometer UV–Visible Evolution™ 260 Bio to determine the electronic transitions associated with functional groups. The morphological features of the samples were analyzed using SEM with a Zeiss Sigma 500 VP analytical FE-SEM instrument from Carl Zeiss Company (Germany). Lastly, the surface area of GQDs, Fe3O4, and GQDs@Fe3O4 was determined using nitrogen adsorption and the BET technique, utilizing Micromeritics equipment (TriStar II 3020 model).

Batch experiments were conducted to assess the adsorption capacity for malathion. A measured quantity of GQDs@Fe3O4 was introduced into 100 mL of malathion solution and stirred at 150 rpm using an orbital shaker (IKA KS control 4000I) until adsorption equilibrium was reached. Subsequently, a neodymium-35 magnet was utilized to separate the adsorbent. The solution's absorbance was determined using UV–Vis spectroscopy. The amount of malathion adsorbed onto the adsorbent surface (Qe, mg g−1) and the malathion removal percentage (E%) were determined using Eqs. (1) and (2) [32].

C0 represents the starting malathion concentration (mg L−1), while Ci refers to its equilibrium concentration (mg L−1). V denotes the volume of the solution (L), and m corresponds to the mass of the adsorbent (g).

To gain insight into the adsorption characteristics of malathion on the adsorbent surface, a comprehensive analysis of the isotherm data is essential. This research employed two distinct isotherm models: Langmuir and Freundlich. The Langmuir model proposes that adsorption occurs as a uniform monolayer on the adsorbent surface, with minimal chemical interactions between the adsorbate molecules and adsorbent surface [33]. Eqs. (3) and (4) mathematically describe the adsorbed amount and separation factor. In contrast, the Freundlich isotherm model describes adsorption as the formation of an adsorbate multilayer on a heterogeneous adsorbent surface. This model addresses various aspects of the adsorption process, including the relationship between increased adsorbed amounts and higher adsorbate concentrations. Eq. (5) provides a mathematical representation of the adsorbed amount according to this model [33].

Here, Qe represents the adsorption capacity (mg g−1), while Qm denotes the theoretical maximum adsorption at monolayer saturation (mg g−1) based on the Langmuir model. KL is the Langmuir constant (L g−1), Ce corresponds to the equilibrium concentration of malathion (mg L−1), and C0 is its initial concentration (mg L−1). The dimensionless separation factor is denoted as RL. Additionally, KF is the Freundlich constant, and n signifies the heteogeneity factor, which reflects the adsorption intensity.

Understanding adsorption kinetics is essential for comprehending reaction rates and the mechanisms involved in the adsorption process. This process includes several stages, such as mass transfer across the boundary layer between the liquid phase and the adsorbent surface, internal diffusion of the adsorbate within the pores of the adsorbent particles, and the formation of physical and chemical bonds between the adsorbate and the adsorbent. These bonds can be formed through van der Waals forces and covalent interactions at the adsorbent's active sites [34]. To thoroughly investigate adsorption kinetics, various mathematical models are applied in the present study to experimental data, including Pseudo-first-order model (PFO), Pseudo-second-order model (PSO), Elovich, and intraparticle diffusion models. These models serve as analytical tools for developing a comprehensive understanding of the adsorption processes. These models are applied using a batch equilibrium system with contact times ranging from 15 to 270 min and initial malathion concentrations between 5 and 120 mg L−1. The PFO model (Eq. (6)) represents the initial adsorption rate and suggests a physisorption process, characterized by weak interactions such as van der Waals forces between the adsorbent and adsorbate. In contrast, the pseudo-second-order model (Eq. (7)) indicates a chemisorption process involving the formation of chemical bonds between the adsorbate and the adsorbent, which results in stronger interactions [35]. The Elovich model (Eq. (8)) provides significant insights into adsorption processes following second-order kinetics by incorporating energetic heterogeneity across the adsorbent surface [36]. This model elucidates the complex nature of chemisorption by considering interactions between adsorbate molecules and active sites on the adsorbent surface [37]. Additionally, the intraparticle diffusion model (Eq. (9)) allows for a comprehensive examination of the diffusion mechanism occurring within the adsorbent material, thereby enhancing molecular-level comprehension of the adsorption process [38].

Qt denotes the amount of malathion adsorbed (mg g−1) at a given time t (min), while Qe represents the adsorption capacity at equilibrium (mg g−1). The parameter k1 corresponds to the rate constant of the PFO kinetic model (min−1), and k2 refers to the rate constant of the PSO kinetic model (g mg−1 min −1). The Elovich model parameters include α, which signifies the initial adsorption rate (g mg−1 min−1), and β, the adsorption constant (mg g−1). Additionally, kp is the intra-particle diffusion rate constant (mg g−1 min-1/2), while C represents the boundary layer thickness-related constant (mg g−1).

For the adsorption of malathion onto GQDs@Fe3O4, thermodynamic parameters were evaluated. Gibbs free energy (ΔG0) at various temperatures was determined using Eq. (10). the van't Hoff equation (Eqs. (11) and (12)) was applied to plot ln Kd against T−1, allowing the standard entropy change (ΔS0) and standard enthalpy change (ΔH0) to be derived from the intercept and slope, respectively.

Qe represents the equilibrium adsorption capacity (mg g−1), while Ce denotes the equilibrium concentration of mlathion in solution (mg L−1). Kd is the adsorption coefficient (L g−1), T corresponds to the adsorption temperature (K), and R is the universal gas constant (8.314 J mol−1 K−1).

The PZC of GQDs@Fe3O4 nanocomposites was determined by dispersing the adsorbent in 100 mL of pH-adjusted DI water for 24 h, followed by measuring the final pH. The PZC was identified as the pH at which no change occurred, indicating a neutral charge on the adsorbent.

The durability of adsorbents in conventional adsorption processes is essential for their efficiency and economic viability, particularly after repeated adsorption-desorption cycles. Accordingly, the regeneration of GQDs@Fe3O4 was performed by dispersing 120 mg of the adsorbent in 100 mL of a mixture of acetone and DI water (1:1 v/v), supplemented with 0.1 M NaOH, followed by agitation for 3 h. The regenerated adsorbent was then collected using a neodymium-35 magnet, and the UV–Vis spectrum of the regeneration solution was obtained to confirm the presence of malathion, indicating the success of the desorption process. This procedure was repeated until no malathion was detected by UV–vis. The regenerated adsorbent was then thoroughly washed with DI water until neutralized and dried under vacuum at 70 °C for 6.5 h, after which it was reutilized under conditions identical to those of the initial adsorption process.

The interaction between malathion and GQDs@Fe3O4 nanocomposites surface was investigated using molecular dynamics (MD) simulation (Fig. S1). The model dimensions for the calculations were set at 22.022 Å × 22.022 Å with a single carbon atom thickness, utilizing the MatS software from BIOVIA. To simulate the aqueous phase, the medium included 1000 water (H2O) molecules along with malathion. The COMPASS III force field was applied to characterize the interactions within the system [39,40]. Energy minimization was carried out using the conjugate gradient method to resolve steric clashes and achieve a local energy minimum. Following NVT equilibration, the system was further equilibrated at 1 atm and 298 K for another 400 ps, employing the Nosé-Hoover thermostat and barostat to regulate system density.

The XRD spectra of GQDs, Fe3O4, and the GQDs@Fe3O4 composite are systematically depicted in Fig. 1 , covering an angular range from 5° to 65°. Fig. 1a illustrates the characteristic XRD pattern of GQDs. A broad peak centered at approximately 2θ = 20° was observed, a feature that can be distinctly attributed to the amorphous carbonaceous structure, thereby indicating the successful synthesis and formation of GQDs [41]. Fig. 1b shows the XRD spectrum of Fe3O4, with prominent diffraction peaks at 2θ = 30.0°, 35.3°, 43.1°, 53.4°, 56.9°, and 62.5°, which can be correlated with the reflections originating from the (220), (311), (4 0 0), (422), (5 1 1), and (4 4 0) crystallographic planes, characteristic of the cubic phase of Fe3O4 nanoparticles [42]. Lastly, Fig. 1c presents the XRD spectrum of GQDs@Fe3O4, which exhibit peaks identical to those of Fe3O4, albeit with slight variations. These include a slight broadening of the peaks at 2θ = 30.0° and 35.3°, as clarified more explicitly in the comparison of XRD patterns showed in Fig. 1d, which can serve as an indication of the interaction with an amorphous carbon structure as presented in the GQDs spectrum. Collectively, these results confirm the successful synthesis of GQDs@Fe3O4 composite.

The FTIR spectra corresponding to the GQDs, Fe3O4, and GQDs@Fe3O4 are shown in Fig. 2 . The FTIR spectrum of the GQDs (Fig. 2a) exhibits a broad band around 3303 cm−1, attributed to OH stretching. A band at 2942 cm−1 corresponds to the aromatic CH stretching, while another at 1646 cm−1 confirms the presence of CO groups in GQDs. Additionally, a band at 1400 cm−1 associated with C-OH stretching vibrations, while the bands at 1200, 1038, and 919 cm−1 were attributed respectively to O-C-O, CO, and CC bonds from epoxy group [43]. Fig. 2b shows the FTIR spectrum of Fe3O4, highlighting a characteristic band of magnetite at 543 cm−1, which is attributed to the FeO bond [44]. Additional bands in the spectrum likely originate from functional groups present in the synthesis. These comprised hydroxyl groups attached to the surface of Fe3O4 that were probably due to water or other hydroxyl containing substances produced during their synthesis [45]. Fig. 2c presents the FTIR spectrum of GQDs@Fe₃O₄, which reveals nearly all bands originating from the GQDs spectrum with slight alterations. These bands, located at 3328, 2921, 1639, 1421, 1237, and 1064 cm−1, are respectively attributed to OH, aromatic CH, CO, C-OH, C-O-C, and CO bonds. Additionally, a distinct band at 543 cm−1 corresponds to the FeO bond, confirming the presence of Fe3O4 nanoparticles. Lastly, Fig. 2d provides a comparative analysis of GQDs, Fe3O4 and GQDs@Fe3O4 spectra. The shaded regions highlight the bands present in the GQDs@Fe3O4 spectrum and their origins from both the GQDs and Fe3O4 spectra. This comparison confirms the successful integration of GQDs with Fe3O4 in the composite material.

Fig. 3a distinctly illustrates the formation and morphology of spherical-shaped iron oxide nanoparticles as observed by SEM, specifically characterized by their notably rough surfaces which exhibit moderate agglomeration in terms of particle distribution, thereby suggesting a relatively uniform dispersion of the nanoparticles. In conjunction with this, the SEM image presented in Fig. 3b showcases the unique characteristics of the GQDs@Fe3O4 composite, wherein the minute spherical Fe3O4 nanoparticles appear somewhat indistinct or blurred due to the uniform application of a delicate coating of GQDs, suggesting that these GQDs are dispersed in a seemingly random manner across the surface of the nanoparticles.

Fig. 4 presents the UV–Vis spectra of GQDs, Fe3O4, and GQDs@Fe3O4. As demonstrated in Fig. 4a, the UV–vis spectrum of the synthesized GQDs exhibits a prominent peak at 270 nm, which is attributed to the π-π* electronic transition originating from the CC bond in the aromatic ring, along with the n-π* electronic transitions [46]. The UV–Visible spectrum of Fe3O4 nanoparticles (Fig. 4b) exhibits a broad absorption band in the range of 330–450 nm (specifically at 351 nm), which is primarily attributed to the absorption and scattering of UV radiation by the magnetic nanoparticles, thus aligning with previously reported findings in the literature [47–49]. The UV–Vis spectrum of GQDs@Fe3O4 (Fig. 4c) exhibits characteristic peaks at 266 and 333 nm. The first peak is attributed to the π-π* and n-π* electronic transitions associated with the GQDs structure, while the second peak at 333 nm originates from Fe3O4. Both peaks exhibit a moderate shift compared to the individual UV–Vis spectra of GQDs (from 270 nm to 266 nm) and Fe3O4 (from 351 nm to 333 nm), as illustrated in Fig. 4d. These spectral shifts collectively suggests the successful synthesis of the GQDs@Fe3O4 composite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of adsorbent amount, contact time, and initial concentration
ce:section.ce:para: The adsorbent dose plays a crucial role in determining the optimal amount needed for effective removal of a specific target. In this study, the influence of the GQDs@Fe3O4 nanocomposite dose on malathion removal was extensively examined by varying its concentration from 5 to 50 mg per 100 mL of malathion solution, with an initial concentration of 5 mg L−1. As illustrated in Fig. 6a, malathion removal efficiency steadily increases with the adsorbent mass, until reaching a saturation point. The observed increase in malathion removal efficiency with increasing adsorbent mass is consistent with the availability of more active sites for adsorption. At lower doses, the limited number of active sites restricts the extent of adsorption, whereas higher doses provide abundant sites, leading to enhanced removal efficiency. The plateau observed beyond 30 mg suggests that all active sites are occupied, and further increments do not contribute significantly to adsorption. As a results, the optimal adsorbent mass was determined to be 30 mg per 100 mL (Qe = 14.53 ± 0.74 mg g−1, E% = 87.19 ± 4.47 %, T = 298.15 K, and pH = 6.3).

To examine the effect of contact time on the adsorption process, 30 mg of GQDs@Fe3O4 was introduced into a 100 mL of malathion solution (with concentrations ranging from 5 to 120 mg L−1) and agitated in an orbital shaker. As shown in Fig. 6b, the results indicate that increasing contact time significantly enhances malathion adsorption until equilibrium is achieved, in approximately 210 min. The initial 60 min displayed a rapid adsorption phase, followed by a more gradual uptake until equilibrium. This initial rapid phase can be attributed to the high specific surface area and abundant active sites on the adsorbent. In contrast, the second phase showed a deceleration in adsorption until equilibrium, likely resulting from the progressive saturation of the adsorbent surface, thereby limiting adsorption process. Which aligns with findings reported in previous studies [51,52].

The initial concentration of pollutants significantly influences the adsorption process, often resulting in enhanced adsorption capacities [53]. To investigate this critical effect, 30 mg of adsorbent was introduced into 100 mL of malathion solution with initial concentrations ranging from 5 to 120 mg L−1. As shown in Fig. 6b, increasing the initial malathion concentration significantly enhances adsorption capacity. The amount adsorbed rises from 14.71 ± 0.50 to 56.64 ± 0.89 mg g−1 as the initial concentration rises from 5 to 120 mg L−1, respectively. The observed enhancement in adsorption capacity with increasing initial malathion concentration can be attributed to a greater driving force for mass transfer, overcoming resistance to diffusion and facilitating the migration of malathion molecules to the adsorbent surface [54].

The pH exerts a pivotal influence, profoundly affecting the active sites of the adsorbent and altering the configuration of pollutants throughout the adsorption mechanism. Initially, prior to delineating the impact of pH on the adsorption phenomenon, the PZC of the adsorbent was determined. As illustrated in Fig. 7a, the surface of GQDs@Fe3O4 exhibited neutrality at a pHPZC = 6.73. To assess the effect of pH on adsorption behavior, a comprehensive examination of adsorption capacity and the removal percentage were executed over a moderate pH spectrum ranging from 3 to 9, as malathion degrades in strongly acidic and alkaline solutions [55]. This exploration of the pH influence was achieved by modulating the initial pH of 100 mL of malathion (5 mg L−1) and 30 mg of adsorbent utilizing HCl (0.1 mol L−1) and NaOH (0.1 mol L−1). The solutions were subjected to agitation in an orbital shaker for 210 min to achieve equilibrium. Fig. 7b demonstrates that the maximum adsorption capacity and the removal percentage occurred at pH = 7 (Qe = 15.10 ± 0.47 mg g−1, E% = 90.60 ± 2.83 % at T = 298.15 K), which is advantageous considering that malathion-contaminated environmental water typically exhibits a pH range between 6 and 8 [56]. The reduction in the adsorption capacity of malathion observed at lower pH values, which are significantly below the levels considered as either natural or moderately alkaline pH, can be attributed to a competitive interaction wherein protons present in acidic conditions for the available adsorption sites against the malathion molecules. This phenomenon can be elucidated by the fact that when the functional groups present within GQDs@Fe3O4 undergo protonation, they subsequently lose their effectiveness and functionality as viable adsorption sites for malathion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ionic strength effect, temperature effect, and adsorption thermodynamics
ce:section.ce:para: The adsorption of pollutants in real water is significantly influenced by the ionic species present, as evidenced by various studies [57]. High conductivity in these water sources, often due to elevated salt concentrations, affects the efficiency of adsorption processes [58]. Herein, experiments were conducted to investigate the effect of ionic strength on malathion adsorption onto GQDs@Fe3O4. Concentrations of NaCl, KCl, and CaCl2 were varied from 0.004 to 0.064 mol L−1 in 100 mL solutions containing 5 mg L−1 of malathion and 30 mg of adsorbent. As shown in Fig. 8a, the adsorption capacity and the removal efficiency decreased with increasing salt concentration. This inhibitory effect is likely due to competition between salt ions and malathion molecules for active sites on the adsorbent surface [59].

The effect of temperature on adsorption is significant, influencing both the capacity and kinetics of the process across various materials and applications [60]. As temperature increases, the adsorption of certain substances can enhance due to changes in molecular conformation and structural rearrangement, while in other cases, lower temperatures may improve adsorption capacities [61,62]. To evaluate this effect on malathion adsorption, four experiments were conducted at temperatures of 298.15, 303.15, 313.15, and 323.15 K under optimized conditions, including 30 mg of adsorbent, an initial malathion concentration of 5 mg L−1, and a 210 min equilibrium time. As demonstrated in Fig. 8b, raising the temperature from 298.15 to 323.15 K significantly increases the adsorption capacity of malathion from 14.53 ± 0.34 to 16.21 ± 0.36 mg g−1, and the removal efficiency from 87.19 ± 2.02 to 97.25 ± 2.19 %. This enhancement can be attributed to increased molecular mobility and reduced solution viscosity at higher temperatures, facilitating interactions between the adsorbent and malathion molecules [61,63].

The thermodynamic analysis of the adsorption process, as presented in Fig. 8c and Table 2 , r a positive standard enthalpy change (ΔH°), confirming the endothermic nature of the adsorption process is endothermic. The observed increase in adsorption capacity with rising temperature (as discussed in section of temperature effect) further supports this conclusion, as higher temperatures facilitate the adsorption process. In contrast, the negative Gibbs free energy values (ΔG°) confirm that the adsorption is thermodynamically favorable and spontaneous under the studied conditions. Moreover, the low positive ΔS° value, calculated from equilibrium data, Suggests that the transition of malathion molecules from the solution phase to the adsorbed state is accompanied by a reduction in molecular randomness (malathion molecules at adsorbed state), and thus reflecting a reduction in the degrees of freedom in the solution phase.

To investigate the adsorption behavior of malathion on the GQDs@Fe3O4 nanocomposite surface, the Langmuir and Freundlich models were applied to analyze the adsorption isotherms (Table 3 and Fig. 9b). The Freundlich model exhibited the highest correlation coefficient, indicating it provides the best fit for the experimental data. This finding suggests that malathion adsorption occurred on a heterogeneous adsorbent surface in a multilayer manner. Furthermore, the structural complexity of GQDs@Fe3O4, with the random position of Fe3O4 nanoparticles in GQDs surface, supports the heterogeneous nature of the adsorption process.

To gain deeper insights into the adsorption kinetics, the data were studied using several kinetic models, including Elovich, PSO, PFO, and intraparticle diffusion models. The PFO model showed the highest correlation (Table 4 and Fig. 9b), suggesting that the adsorption of malathion onto the GQDs@Fe3O4 nanocomposites surface is primarily governed by a physisorption mechanism [64]. Although the PSO and Elovich models were also considered, they exhibited a poorer fit to the data, indicating that chemisorption and heterogeneous surface adsorption mechanisms are not the dominant processes in this case [35,65]. The intraparticle diffusion model was also evaluated, but the results didn't reveal a distinct multi-stage adsorption pattern, confirming that intraparticle diffusion is not the rate-limiting step in this adsorption process [38].

Following the regeneration procedure described in the experimental section, GQDs@Fe3O4 nanocomposites were regenerated and reused for three cycles, as shown in Fig. 10 . The findings indicate that even at the third cycle, the adsorbent retained 70.64 ± 5.07 % of the efficacy observed during the initial use.

Malathion is extensively utilized in agriculture for the control of insect pests such as aphids, leafhoppers, and whiteflies. While it serves to protect fruits, vegetables, and field crops from pest-induced damage, it is a prevalent pollutant, particularly in rivers or reservoirs proximal to these crops, potentially impacting aquatic organisms and ecosystems [66]. Consequently, an investigation into its efficacy in analogous real water sources was conducted to utilize the results as a foundation for predicting the outcomes achievable in practical applications. To this end, a comparative adsorption experiment was conducted in distilled, dam, and river water, each spiked with 80 mg L−1 under optimal conditions. The results of this experiment are presented in Fig. 11 . In distilled water, which simulates laboratory ideal conditions, GQDs@Fe3O4 showed an adsorption capacity of 43.95 ± 7.79 mg g−1. However, the adsorbent's efficacy decreased by 27 % (Qe = 31.96 ± 6.22 mg g−1) in dam water and by 33 % (Qe = 29.26 ± 7.05 mg g−1) in river water. Overall, the adsorbent's performance was slightly reduced. These findings highlight the robustness of the adsorbent even in more challenging, non-ideal water sources and suggest its potential applicability for real-world water treatment processes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The existence of malathion molecules on the adsorbent surface and the theoretical investigation of adsorption mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: The adsorption experiments are based on quantifying the decrease in pollutant concentration in the solution, expressed as adsorption capacity. However, to confirm the adsorption, it's essential to characterize the surface functional groups after the process to verify the presence of malathion and its interaction with the adsorbent surface. Fig. 12 presents a comparison of the FTIR spectra of the adsorbent before and after the adsorption process, highlighting notable modifications that occur following malathion adsorption. Firstly, after adsorption, two new bands were detected at 1738 and 1037 cm−1, attributed to the CO stretching of the ester group and the P-OCH3 bond resulting from malathion molecules present on the surface of the adsorbent [56,67]. Secondly, the bands observed at 2921, 1421, and 1237 cm−1, corresponding to aromatic CH, C-OH, and C-O-C, respectively, in the spectrum before adsorption, diminished or nearly disappeared following adsorption, which may serve as an indication of the interaction between these functional groups and the malathion molecules that are adsorbed onto the surface of GQDs@Fe3O4. Collectively, these observations confirm the adsorption process and suggest that the decrease in pollutant concentration in the solution results from its adsorption onto the adsorbent surface.

MD simulations have emerged as a powerful contemporary tool for researching interactions at various scales, including those between molecules, polymers, metal inhibitors, and biopolymers [68–70]. Researchers may examine the structural and functional characteristics of molecules in a range of environments thanks to these simulations, which provide deep insights into the dynamic behavior and interaction processes of complex systems. In this section, using MD simulations, we will attempt to investigate and get a better understanding of the adsorption behavior of the malathion molecule on the surface of the GQDs@Fe3O4 in aqueous phases. Fig. 13 depicts the leading cells of malathion molecule on the nanostructure of GQDs@Fe3O4 surface in the aqueous phases, with the temperature set at 298 K. The results showed that the majority of the active areas of malathion interact with the surface of GQDs@Fe3O4, where its shape enables it to take on a planar orientation. The (-OH) and (-COOH) groups on GQDs@Fe3O4 establish hydrogen bonds with the ester (-COOEt) and thiophosphoryl (-PS) groups present in the structure of malathion. Additionally, by interacting with the unsaturated bonds in malathion, the π-conjugated system of GQDs@Fe3O4 helps to stabilize its adsorption. Malathion's alkyl chains enhance GQDs@Fe3O4 ' adsorption by aligning with its hydrophobic regions. Furthermore, stable surface complexes are formed as a result of the great binding affinity of the -PS group in malathion to the Fe atoms in Fe3O4. Table 1S lists and calculates the energetic characteristics (EBinding and EInteraction) of adsorbed malathion on the surface of GQDs@Fe3O4 in aqueous solutions. It is vital to note that in aqueous phases, a larger positive value of EBinding and a higher negative value of EInteraction suggest more stable/easier adsorption onto the GQDs@Fe3O4 surface. The energy estimates indicate that in aqueous phases, the title molecule interacts significantly with the GQDs@Fe3O4 surface (Table 1S). Furthermore, the EInteraction and EBinding values are −6471.094 kcal mol−1 and 6471.094 kcal mol−1, respectively, (Table 1S). Overall, the MD results suggest that malathion has a high adsorption capacity on GQDs@Fe3O4 in aqueous phases. These data confirm the experiment's results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance and comparison of the efficacy of GQDs@Fe3O4 nanocomposites for the removal of malathion with other adsorbents
ce:section.ce:para: The adsorption of malathion from aqueous solutions has been extensively studied using various adsorbents, with different maximum malathion concentrations ranging from 16 to 120 mg L−1 and under various adsorption conditions, including adsorbent dose, pH, and temperature. A comparison of the efficacy of malathion adsorption, considering several factors into accounts, is presented in Table 5 . As an initial point of reference, Kumar et al. employed powdered activated carbon (PAC) with an optimal adsorbent dose of 2000 mg L−1, achieving a maximum adsorption capacity of 21.74 mg g−1 at an malathion concentration of 50 mg L−1 [71]. This capacity was attained at an optimal temperature of 318.15 K, where elevated temperature was observed to positively influence the adsorption response. Likewise, Hosseini et al. successfully developed a sodium alginate/biosilicate/magnetite nanocomposites an adsorbent for malathion removal at an initial concentration of 100 mg L−1 [72]. They achieved a maximum adsorption capacity of 36.86 mg g−1 with an optimal adsorbent dose of 2500 mg L−1 at a temperature of 318.15 K, noting that increased temperature positively influenced adsorption. In contrast, Kalantary et al. synthesized magnetic graphene oxide for malathion uptake from water, attaining a notable adsorption capacity of 43.29 mg g−1 at an malathion concentration of 75 mg L−1 and a temperature of 293.15 K, where a decrease in temperature was found to enhance adsorption, in opposition to the previous findings [56]. However, this favorable adsorption capacity was achieved with a higher adsorbent dose of 4800 mg L−1, which may be considered a potentially unfavorable outcome. In the present study, GQDs@Fe3O4 was fabricated and used as an adsorbent for malathion uptake, achieving a high maximum adsorption capacity of 86.02 ± 2.74 mg g−1 at an initial malathion concentration of 120 mg L−1 and an optimal temperature of 323.15 K. Notably, it also demonstrated a high adsorption capacity at ambient temperature (298.15 K), reaching 56.63 ± 0.89 mg g−1. It is emphasized that these findings were obtained with a low adsorbent dose of 300 mg L−1.

In summary, this study demonstrates the development of GQDs derived from olive pomace waste as a waste valorization strategy, functionalized with Fe3O4 to facilitate adsorbent recovery and utilized as an efficient adsorbent for malathion removal from environmental water, including river and dam water. The successful synthesis of GQDs@Fe3O4 was confirmed using SEM, FTIR, XRD, and Uv-Vis. The adsorption performance was systematically evaluated under various operational conditions, revealing that 30 mg of GQDs@Fe3O4, with 210 min equilibrium time, achieved a removal efficiency of 87.19 ± 2.02 % at T = 298.15 K, which increased to E% = 97.24 ± 2.19 % at T = 323.15 K, for a 5 mg L−1 malathion concentration. The adsorption process followed the PFO kinetic model and was best described by the Freundlich isotherm. Thermodynamic analysis confirmed that the adsorption process was spontaneous, endothermic, and thermodynamically favorable. The capacity to regenerate and reutilize the adsorbent was evaluated, with optimal malathion recovery attained utilizing a specific eluent. The GQDs@Fe3O4 nanocomposite exhibited efficient adsorption of malathion residues and demonstrated potential for practical environmental applications in the remediation of pesticide-contaminated water sources.
